attributes.stem: A three-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of fever, irritability, and widespread skin redness. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date. His parents report that the skin rash started as a few small red lesions on the face, axillae and groin, but it quickly spread to involve the entire body. On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 130/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 39.3°C. His skin is erythematous with numerous superficial blisters, which coalesce to form large, thin-walled blisters that easily rupture upon touch. The skin surface appears to peel off easily. There are no signs of mucosal involvement.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely causative organism of this condition?
attributes.explanation:
The patient in this scenario has most likely presented with Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS). The characteristic signs and symptoms include fever, diffuse erythematous rash, and tenderness with skin peeling, commonly starting around the mouth and flexure areas and spreading to the rest of the body. Staphylococcus aureus is the most likely causative organism of SSSS. The exfoliative toxins produced by certain strains of S. aureus lead to the characteristic skin peeling and blistering seen in SSSS. It is important to note that not all strains of S. aureus produce these toxins, so not all staphylococcal infections cause SSSS.

Streptococcus pyogenes is associated with other skin conditions, such as impetigo, cellulitis, and necrotizing fasciitis, but not SSSS. The presentation of SSSS and skin infections from S. pyogenes can be similar, however this presentation is more in keeping with SSSS.

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is known to cause various infections, including skin and soft tissue infections in immunocompromised individuals, but it is not the causative organism of SSSS.

Staphylococcus epidermidis is a common skin commensal and is associated with infections related to indwelling medical devices or prosthetic implants, such as prosthetic joint infections or catheter-associated infections. It is not typically responsible for SSSS.

Clostridium perfringens is a bacterium commonly associated with gas gangrene. It produces toxins that can cause severe tissue destruction and necrosis, particularly in deep tissue infections. However, it is not implicated in SSSS.

The diagnosis of SSSS is primarily clinical, based on the characteristic appearance of the skin and the patient's age. Treatment typically involves intravenous antibiotics effective against S. aureus, fluid management, pain control, and meticulous wound care. In severe cases, admission to a burn unit may be necessary for optimal management of the extensive skin involvement.
learningPoints: Staphylococcus aureus is responsible for causing Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS).
SSSS typically presents with fever, diffuse erythema, and superficial blistering that easily ruptures.
SSSS is caused by exfoliative toxins produced by certain strains of Staphylococcus aureus.
references: Brazel M, Desai A, Are A, Motaparthi K. Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome and Bullous Impetigo. Medicina (Kaunas). 2021;57(11):1157. Published 2021 Oct 24. doi:10.3390/medicina57111157
